segments.caption: The video opens with a serene night scene featuring a large, glowing full moon rising over a dark landscape with silhouetted trees, setting a mysterious and tranquil tone. The scene quickly shifts to a vibrant, colorful animated world where a blonde woman drives a pink convertible through a sunny, palm-tree-lined suburban street, suggesting a whimsical or fantastical setting. This transitions to a tense, dimly lit interior where a man in a red vest appears to be in a confrontation, followed by a dramatic shot of a man in a dark coat standing atop a futuristic, illuminated structure against a dark sky, hinting at a sci-fi or action narrative. The sequence then cuts to a group of armored figures walking through a fiery, war-torn environment, emphasizing danger and conflict. A close-up of a man in a suit at a table, possibly in a tense negotiation, is followed by a surreal, animated explosion of red energy in a dark room, suggesting a supernatural or magical event. The mood shifts to warmth and intimacy with a close-up of a smiling couple, then to a dramatic, animated scene of a man in a red and white outfit carrying a child through a lush, sunlit landscape. The video then shows a man in a white shirt lying on a bed, seemingly in distress, before cutting to a group of people joyfully splashing in water, conveying liberation and celebration. This is followed by a man standing triumphantly on a snowy mountain peak, arms raised, symbolizing victory or achievement. The tone darkens again with a black-and-white shot of a woman in a vintage setting, looking concerned, then shifts to a chaotic, neon-lit cityscape with people running in panic, possibly during an explosion or disaster. A dramatic, dark scene shows a figure falling through a stormy sky, followed by a close-up of a woman in a white dress with a hand gently touching her shoulder, suggesting comfort or connection. The final frames depict a woman in a futuristic, mechanical headpiece, possibly undergoing a transformation or experiment, and end with a man in a dark suit standing in a dimly lit room, surrounded by glowing objects, hinting at a mysterious or technological plot. The sequence moves rapidly between genres—fantasy, sci-fi, drama, and action—creating a dynamic and emotionally charged narrative arc.

The clips showcase diverse animation styles—from hand-drawn to 3D—and dynamic scenes including a boy with a heron, elemental characters interacting, a superhero battle, a robot and dog in a forest, and Spider-Man swinging through a vibrant, stylized city. The montage culminates with the title 'ANIMATED FEATURE FILM' appearing on screen. Returning to the stage, the presenters announce the winner, and a woman in a shimmering light-pink gown walks forward to accept the Oscar, holding it with both hands. The presenters applaud as she steps onto the stage, and the audience is visible in the background, clapping. segments.caption: The segment opens with two women standing at a podium on a stage with a soft, abstract purple and white backdrop. The Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, with long dark hair and red-painted nails, gestures expressively as she speaks, while the Woman in Red Gown with Magenta Cape, wearing a vibrant red dress with a dramatic magenta cape and puffed sleeves, holds a beige envelope labeled 'ORIGINAL SCREENPLAY' with a gold seal, smiling and nodding in response. The scene transitions to a series of title cards on crumpled paper, each introducing a film: 'ANATOMY OF A FALL' by Justine Triet and Arthur Harari, followed by a clip of a woman named Sandra in a purple shirt saying, 'You are not a victim. Not at all. Your generosity conceals something dirtier and meaner.' Next, 'THE HOLDOVERS' by David Hemingson is shown, with a clip of a student named Kountze in a classroom saying, 'I can't fail this,' and a teacher named Paul responding, 'Don't sell yourself short, Mr. Kountze. I truly believe that you can.' Then, 'MAESTRO' by Bradley Cooper & Josh Singer appears, with a black-and-white clip of a man named LB saying, 'It's too much, isn't it? Too much. I love too much, what can I say, but I'm reigning it in.' Finally, 'MAY DECEMBER' by Samy Burch and Alex Mechanik is introduced, with a brief clip of a blonde woman in sunglasses, identified as Grace, speaking. The sequence functions as a montage of nominated screenplays, each introduced with a title card and a representative dialogue clip, creating a narrative flow that highlights the emotional and thematic depth of each film.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a somber, introspective conversation between two men, Monk and Cliff, in a dimly lit interior. Monk, a bald man with a beard and glasses, wearing a white shirt, asks, 'Does seeing a dead body ever become normal?' Cliff, a man with short black hair and a beard, also in a white shirt, responds, 'I don’t know. I haven’t seen many.' Monk expresses surprise with 'Really?' and Cliff clarifies, 'I’m a plastic surgeon. If I’m looking at a corpse, then something went very awry.' The scene transitions to a title card for 'THE ZONE OF INTEREST,' written by Jonathan Glazer and based on a novel by Martin Amis. The next scene shows a suburban setting with a child riding a toy car on a gravel path, while a woman named Linna asks another, Hedwig, 'Jews in the house?' Hedwig replies, 'The Jews are over the other side of the wall. They’re local. Das sind Mädchen.' She then instructs children, 'Say hello to Grandma.' The video cuts to a title card for 'OPPENHEIMER,' written by Christopher Nolan and based on a book by Kai Bird and Martin J. Sherwin. A group of men crouch behind a wooden barrier as a massive explosion erupts in the desert. The scene shifts to a dimly lit room where a man in a suit, identified as Bohr, says, 'Algebra’s like sheet music. The important thing isn’t can you read music, it’s can you hear it. Can you hear the music, Robert?' The final segment opens with a title card for 'BARBIE,' written by Greta Gerwig & Noah Baumbach and based on Barbie by Mattel. In a vibrant, pink-hued room, a woman named Gloria, wearing a pink polka-dot blouse and pink pants, expresses exhaustion, saying, 'I’m just so tired of watching myself and every single thing I do.'

segments.caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a grand, dimly lit auditorium bathed in purple and pink hues, where an audience is seated and clapping enthusiastically. The scene transitions to a stage with a circular, illuminated backdrop, where two men walk confidently toward the center. One is The Rock, wearing a shiny silver suit with a maroon scarf and a silver chain, while the other is a man in a black double-breasted suit with glasses, holding a card labeled 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE'. The camera then cuts to a wide shot of the Oscars Stage, where the category 'SUPPORTING ACTOR' is displayed in large, illuminated letters above a golden Oscar statue. The Man in Dark Suit with Glasses, dressed in a maroon tuxedo with a black bow tie and glasses, stands at the podium holding an envelope, announcing the nominees. A graphic appears on screen listing the five nominees: Sterling K. Brown, Robert Downey Jr., Robert De Niro, Ryan Gosling, and Mark Ruffalo, each shown in a circular frame with their name below. As the winner is announced, Robert Downey Jr. reacts with surprise, covering his mouth, then quickly stands and rushes down the aisle through the applauding Oscars Audience. He runs energetically across the stage, waving to the crowd, and is greeted by the Man in Dark Suit with Glasses, who hands him the Oscar. Robert Downey Jr. then approaches the podium, where a digital nameplate reads 'ROBERT DOWNEY JR. ACTOR IN A SUPPORTING ROLE OPPENHEIMER'. He stands at the microphone, holding the Oscar, and begins his acceptance speech with a composed and emotional expression, his black tuxedo with a black shirt and a small white pin on the lapel clearly visible against the blue draped backdrop.

segments.caption: The video begins with a cinematic montage of nominated films for Best Original Score, each displayed through a stylized piano-key visual effect. The first clip features 'AMERICAN FICTION' with Laura Karpmans score, showing a man in a white shirt and another in a blue jacket shaking hands in a sunlit room. This transitions to 'INDIANA JONES AND THE DIAL OF DESTINY' with John Williams' score, depicting an older man in glasses and a hat examining a golden artifact, followed by a vintage car driving through a dusty street. Next, 'KILLERS OF THE FLOWER MOON' with Robbie Robertson's score is shown, featuring a man in a suit and hat walking through a 1920s town, then sitting in a dimly lit saloon. The montage continues with 'OPPENHEIMER' by Ludwig Göransson, showing a man in a checkered jacket in a car, then a close-up of a man in a suit with a serious expression. Finally, 'POOR THINGS' by Jerskin Fendrix is displayed, with a woman in a yellow dress walking through an ornate, warmly lit interior, followed by a man in a dark shirt holding a glass. The montage concludes with a grand reveal of the 'ORIGINAL SCORE' category on a golden, circular stage. Two presenters, Da’Vine Joy Randolph in a striking emerald green gown with dramatic ruffled shoulders and Ariana Grande in a voluminous pink strapless gown with her blonde hair in an elegant updo, stand at the podium. They open the envelope and announce the winner. The audience erupts in applause as Ludwig Göransson, dressed in a black suit, rises from his seat, smiling and clapping, and walks down the aisle through the cheering crowd. He reaches the stage, where he is greeted by the presenters, and receives the Oscar statuette with a joyful expression, as the audience continues to applaud.

The scene cuts to the audience, where Billie Eilish, in a black blazer over a white blouse and a checkered skirt, and Finneas O’Connell, in a black tuxedo with a red boutonniere, react with surprise and joy as their name is called for 'What Was I Made For?' from 'Barbie'. They rise from their seats, hugging and celebrating with friends and colleagues, including a woman in a black sequined blazer and a man in a black tuxedo. The crowd erupts in applause as they make their way down the aisle, embracing others along the way. Upon reaching the stage, they are greeted with hugs from Da’Vine Joy Randolph and Ariana Grande. Billie and Finneas stand at the podium, holding their Oscars, with Billie speaking into the microphone. A lower-third graphic appears, reading: 'WHAT WAS I MADE FOR? BILLIE EILISH, FINNEAS O’CONNELL ORIGINAL SONG BARBIE'. The atmosphere is celebratory and emotional, with warm golden lighting illuminating the stage and the audience’s enthusiastic reactions.

segments.caption: Billie Eilish and Finneas O’Connell stand at the Oscars podium, having just won the award for Best Original Song for 'What Was I Made For?' from the film Barbie. Billie, dressed in a black blazer over a white ruffled blouse with a red pin on her lapel, holds the Oscar statuette and speaks emotionally into the microphone, her expression shifting between gratitude and tears. Finneas, in a black tuxedo with a red boutonniere, stands beside her, listening intently and occasionally glancing at her with a supportive smile. The stage is bathed in warm golden light with elegant curved arches in the background. As Billie speaks, the camera cuts to the audience, showing Greta Gerwig and Margot Robbie clapping and smiling warmly. Billie then turns to Finneas, who takes the microphone and delivers a brief, heartfelt speech, gesturing with his hands and sharing a moment of joy with her. The pair exchange smiles and a light laugh, clearly moved by the moment. The on-screen graphic at the bottom of the screen displays the title of the winning song, the artists' names, and the film it’s from. The atmosphere is celebratory and intimate, capturing a genuine moment of shared triumph between the siblings.

segments.caption: The video captures a dynamic and emotional moment at the Oscars as Cillian Murphy, dressed in a classic black tuxedo with a white pleated shirt and a gold circular lapel pin, is announced as the winner for Best Actor in a Leading Role for 'Oppenheimer'. He is initially seen in the audience, reacting with wide-eyed surprise and an open-mouthed expression of disbelief. He rises and is embraced warmly by a woman in a sparkling silver gown, identified as Greta Gerwig, who smiles and claps joyfully. He then moves through the standing, applauding audience, hugging another man in a white tuxedo, identified as Robert Downey Jr., who beams with pride. As he walks toward the stage, he is greeted by a bald man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, identified as the Presenter in Black Tuxedo, who hands him the Oscar statuette. Murphy then walks onto the circular stage, which is illuminated with purple drapes and a large golden Oscar statue in the background. He approaches the podium, visibly emotional, placing a hand on his face and then gesturing expressively as he begins his acceptance speech. The audience, including notable figures like the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses and the Man in White Tuxedo, continues to applaud. A lower-third graphic appears, identifying him as 'CILLIAN MURPHY, ACTOR IN A LEADING ROLE, OPPENHEIMER'. The atmosphere is one of celebration, with warm stage lighting and a sea of clapping guests in formal attire.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a montage of behind-the-scenes footage from nominated films, showcasing Christopher Nolan on the set of *Oppenheimer* with a large IMAX camera in a desert landscape, Yorgos Lanthimos directing a scene from *Poor Things* in an ornate interior, and Jonathan Glazer filming *The Zone of Interest* in a suburban garden. The scene transitions to the Oscars stage, where the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses, identified as the presenter, stands before a large golden Oscar statue and purple drapes, announcing the nominees for Best Director. The nominees—Justine Triet, Martin Scorsese, Christopher Nolan, Yorgos Lanthimos, and Jonathan Glazer—are displayed on a circular graphic beside him. The audience erupts in applause as Christopher Nolan rises from his seat, walks down the aisle, embracing colleagues like Robert Downey Jr. and Cillian Murphy along the way. He reaches the stage, where he is warmly greeted by the presenter with a hug and a kiss on the cheek. Nolan accepts the Oscar statuette, holding it with both hands, and stands at the podium, visibly moved, as the on-screen text confirms his win: 'CHRISTOPHER NOLAN, DIRECTING, OPPENHEIMER.' The atmosphere is celebratory, with the audience clapping and the stage illuminated in warm golden light.

segments.caption: The video opens with Tim Robbins, dressed in a dark suit, standing at a podium on a golden-lit stage, holding a golden envelope and reading from a card. He announces the winner of Best Picture, then hands the envelope to the presenter beside him. The camera cuts to the audience, where Christopher Nolan, in a black tuxedo, rises from his seat, holding his Oscar statuette, and begins walking down the aisle. He is followed by a group of cast and crew members, including Cillian Murphy, Robert Downey Jr., and Emma Stone, all in formal attire, clapping and celebrating. As they make their way through the audience, they are greeted with enthusiastic applause and hugs from fellow attendees, including Martin Scorsese, who is seen smiling and clapping. The group ascends the stage, where they gather around the podium, embracing each other and sharing joyful moments. The stage is grand, with a large golden Oscar statue backdrop and the words 'BEST PICTURE' illuminated above. The audience continues to cheer as the winners celebrate their victory, with the camera capturing wide shots of the entire auditorium filled with clapping guests. The atmosphere is electric, filled with excitement and camaraderie as the team celebrates their win.

segments.caption: The video captures the triumphant moment when the cast and crew of *Oppenheimer* accept the Best Picture Oscar on stage. The group, dressed in formal black-tie attire, gathers on a circular stage adorned with golden drapes and pink floral arrangements, with a massive golden Oscar statue as the backdrop. Emma Thomas, in a shimmering black sequined gown with sheer polka-dotted sleeves and green earrings, steps forward to speak at the podium, holding her Oscar statuette. She is flanked by Charles Roven and Christopher Nolan, both in classic black tuxedos, each holding their own Oscars. The camera alternates between wide shots of the entire group and close-ups of Emma Thomas as she delivers an emotional, joyful speech, gesturing warmly and smiling broadly. The on-screen graphic identifies the winners as 'EMMA THOMAS, CHARLES ROVEN, CHRISTOPHER NOLAN' for 'BEST PICTURE OPPENHEIMER'. The atmosphere is celebratory, with the audience clapping and the stage bathed in warm golden light, emphasizing the grandeur of the moment. The group stands together, beaming, as the camera pans across their faces, capturing their shared elation and pride.

segments.caption: The video segment presents a rapid succession of Oscar winners accepting their awards on stage, each framed within a golden border against a textured gold background. The sequence begins with Dave Mullins and Brad Booker, both in black tuxedos, accepting the Animated Short Film award for 'War Is Over! Inspired by the Music of John & Yoko,' with Mullins holding the Oscar and Booker standing beside him. Next, Nadia Stacey, Mark Coulier, and Josh Weston, dressed in formal attire, accept the Makeup and Hairstyling award for 'Poor Things,' with Stacey in a shimmering silver dress and Coulier in a black tuxedo. James Price and Shona Heath, in a black tuxedo and a light green coat with a red scarf respectively, accept the Production Design award for 'Poor Things.' Holly Waddington, in a dark, embellished gown, accepts the Costume Design award for 'Poor Things.' Takashi Yamazaki, Kiyoko Shibuya, Masaki Takahashi, and Tatsuji Nojima, in formal wear, accept the Visual Effects award for 'Godzilla Minus One.' Jennifer Lame, in a green strapless gown, accepts the Film Editing award for 'Oppenheimer.' Ben Proudfoot and Kris Bowers, in a black tuxedo and a light blue dress respectively, accept the Documentary Short Film award for 'The Last Repair Shop.' Mstyslav Chernov, Michelle Mizner, and Raney Aronson-Rath, in formal black attire, accept the Documentary Feature Film award for '20 Days in Mariupol.' Hoyte van Hoytema, in a black tuxedo, accepts the Cinematography award for 'Oppenheimer,' followed by a brief clip from 'The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar' showing a man in a suit running through a grand, sunlit hall. Tarn Willers and Johnnie Burn, in a black tuxedo and a black suit with a hat respectively, accept the Sound award for 'The Zone of Interest.' The segment concludes with end credits rolling over the gold background, listing Executive Producers Katy Mulan and Raj Kapoor, Co-Executive Producer Rob Paine, Director Hamish Hamilton, and a long list of writers including Jimmy Kimmel, Carol Leifer, and Molly McNearney.

The text is white, centered, and presented in a clean, sans-serif font. The credits progress sequentially, listing key production roles and individuals involved in the event. The sequence begins with the Producers: Taryn Hurd, Sarah Levine Hall, Erin Erwin, and Jennifer Sharron. Dickinson and Noah Mitz, and Associate Directors Hayley Collett, Susan Kopensky, Lori Margules, Robin Mishkin Abrams, Sara Niimi, Michael Polito, and Kristen Patterson Terry. The Head Stage Manager Gary Natoli is listed next, followed by International Associate Producer Carol Scandaro, International Production Manager Chris Bisch, and International Post Production Supervisor Booy Kober.
